Introduction and Review of
Probability Theory Course Goals:
вҖў Review a very broad collection of methods so you are
introduced to the most fundamental statistical
procedures.
вҖў Provide you with both theoretical and practical
background, including real data examples.
вҖў Understand the assumptions and limitations of
statistical methods.
вҖў Being able to select the appropriate method for
analyzing a specific dataset.

What is Statistics?
вҖў A branch of mathematics that learns from data
вҖў Pause: Then what is machine learning??
вҖў Applied statistics: concerned with methods for collecting,
analyzing, and drawing conclusions from data.
вҖў Theoretical statistics: establishes the properties and conditions
under which the data analysis methods work.
вҖў Without rudimentary knowledge of how it works, people can't
make evaluations of a wide variety of numerical encounters in
daily life.
вҖў Many statistical methods are relevant and useful in a wide
range of academic and non-academic disciplines, beyond just
the statistical and data sciences.

Key idea in statistics:
explain the variation
вҖў To do this we need to express scientifically the variation in the data
as observations of random variables and use a statistical model to
explain it (at least partially). Statistical analysis requires data,
which may be viewed as an observed sample from a population.
вҖў The data X , вҖҰ , X are considered as n, usually independent and
1 n
identically distributed, random variables with a common
population distribution/model. This population can be specified as
a formula with either its cumulative distribution function (cdf) F(x)
or its probability density function (pdf) f(x).

вҖў Let a typical height of the cross-pollinated plant be denoted X

Lecture Goals:
Define
estimators

of estimators Parametric vs. Nonparametric Models
вҖў Our data are X , вҖҰ , X which are iid random sample from a
1 n
population with a pdf f(x) or cdf F(x).
вҖў Very often the population distribution is not completely known,
and f(x) depends on an unknown parameter рқңғ, which can be
indicated with f(x; рқңғ).
вҖў When f(x) depends on an unknown parameter рқңғ, we say that we
are considering a parametric model for our data.
вҖў Example: linear regression is a parametric model.
вҖў Non-parametric methods are statistical techniques that do not rely
on specific assumptions about the underlying distribution of the
population being studied.
вҖў Example: K-Nearest Neighbor is a nonparametric model.
вҖў In this course we focus on the parametric models.

Interpretation
Q: What does 95% confidence mean?
A: The procedure we are using to construct the interval
is 95% reliable. That is, 95% of all the intervals
constructed with this method will cover the unknown
value of рқңҮ, and 5% will miss it.
Therefore, we donвҖҷt say рқңҮ вҲҲ (l, u) with probability
95%, because рқңҮ is not a random variable! The best way
express the interpretation is to say:
We are 95% confident that рқңҮ is between l and u.

Interpretation Note on the width of the CI
The width of the confidence interval, that is, its
accuracy depends on:
вҖў The sample size n: the larger the sample size the
narrow the width of the CI.
вҖў The confidence level: the higher the confidence the
wider the CI will be!
вҖў The standard deviation of the population or SE: the
larger the SE the wider the CI will be.
вҖў The method used to construct the CI

Resampling Methods
Involves repeatedly drawing samples from a training set and
в–Ә
refitting a model or estimator of interest on each sample to obtain
more information about the fitted model/estimator.
Example: We can estimate the variability of a linear regression fit
в–Ә
by repeatedly drawing different samples from the training data,
fitting a OLS regression to each new sample, and then examining
the extent to which the resulting fits differ.
Model Assessment: having chosen a final model, estimating its
в–Ә
prediction error on new data.
Model Selection: estimating the performance of different models
в–Ә
in order to choose the best one.

If we are in a data-rich situation, the best approach for both model

Problems With a Single Validation
Set
Beyond the variability identified in the last plot, another concern is that
в–Ә
only a subset of the observations are used in the model training.
In our case, we used 50 instead of 100 observations to fit the model.
в–Ә
Most statistical methods perform more poorly when they are trained on
в–Ә
fewer observations, so use of a single validation set may lead to
overestimation of the error rate.
Cross-validation is an extension of the validation set approach we just
в–Ә
discussed that can address both issues:
1. Cross-validation averages error rates over multiple samples (i.e.,
resampling) to address the variability of MSEs.
2. Cross-validation fits may be carried out with as many as all but
one of the observations in a data set, thereby minimizing the
extent to which we overestimate error rate due to lack of sufficient
sample size relative to the full sample.

Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
Instead of creating two subsets of comparable size, a single
в–Ә
observation is used for the validation set and the remaining
observations (n вҖ“ 1) make up the training set.

вҖ“ Fit the model using the training data set
вҖ“ Evaluate the model using validation set and
compute the corresponding MSE.

K-Fold Cross-Validation
Probably the simplest and most widely used method for estimating
в–Ә
prediction error.
This method directly estimates the average prediction error when the
в–Ә
machine learning method is applied to an independent test sample.
Ideally, if we had enough data, we would set aside a validation set (as
в–Ә
previously described) and use it to assess the performance of our
prediction model.
To finesse the problem, K-fold cross-validation uses part of the available
в–Ә
data to fit the model, and a different part to test it.

K-Fold Cross-Validation (cont.)
We use this method because LOOCV is
в–Ә
computationally intensive/slow.
We randomly divide the data set of into
в–Ә
K folds (typically K = 5 or 10).
One of the folds is treated as a test set, and the method is fit on the
в–Ә
remaining K вҖ“ 1 folds. The MSPE is computed on the observations
in the held-out fold. The process is repeated K times, taking out a
different part each time.
By averaging the K estimates of the test error, we get an estimated
в–Ә
validation (test) error rate for new observations.

The bootstrap is a flexible and powerful statistical tool that
в–Ә

estimator or statistical method; it is a general tool for

As an example, the bootstrap can be used to estimate the
в–Ә

fit, or a confidence interval for that coefficient.
The use of the term bootstrap derives from the phrase вҖңto
в–Ә

promotions at banks?
A: One way to test this
hypothesis is by reshuffling the
sample.

Motivating example:
Does gender affect promotions at a bank?
вҖў Try to imagine a hypothetical universe
where no gender discrimination in
promotions existed. In such a
hypothetical universe, the gender of an
applicant would have no bearing on
their chances of promotion.
вҖў Bringing things back to our promotions
data frame, the gender variable would
thus be an irrelevant label.
вҖў If these gender labels were irrelevant,
then we could randomly reassign them
by вҖңshufflingвҖқ them to no consequence!
вҖў It appears the difference in вҖңmale
namesвҖқ versus вҖңfemale namesвҖқ
promotion rates is now different.

Motivating example:
Does gender affect promotions at a bank?
вҖў The final step is to measure how
surprised we are by a promotion
difference of 29.2% in a
hypothesized universe of no
gender discrimination. If the
observed difference of 0.292 is
highly unlikely, then we would
be inclined to reject the validity
of our hypothesized universe.
вҖў This is known as a permutation
test. You can use it to quantify
the probability the original
labeling was random or not by
reshuffling many times.
вҖў It is a nonparametric method. In
this course we focus on
parametric hypothesis testing.
